headline: US tariff reset after Supreme Court ruling leaves Malaysia worse off
context: The US Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs in February 2026, but Section 301 investigations now target solar, steel, aluminium and EVs across 60 economies. Malaysia walked out of the ART deal with no bilateral framework — 68% of its chip exports come from US firms.

cards.big: Malaysia celebrated the Supreme Court striking down Trump's tariffs. The replacement regime may be worse.
cards.sub: SCOTUS voided the 24% IEEPA tariff and the ART deal. Trump imposed a 10% blanket tariff within days and launched two Section 301 probes — legally unchallengeable, with no rate cap.

cards.big: IEEPA tariffs had a constitutional weakness. Section 301 tariffs do not — court challenges are nearly impossible.
cards.sub: Section 301 targets solar, steel, aluminium and EVs — not chips directly. But Malaysia's finished electronics could fall in scope. The White House exempted computers, phones and SSDs.

cards.big: 68% of semiconductor exports come from US firms. The 180,000 ATP workers are 65% women. R&D flatlined at 1% of GDP for a decade.
cards.sub: RM60.6B in chips within RM120.2B E&E exports to US. NSSP commits RM25B, but Silterra — the one wafer-fab attempt — failed. The CHIPS Act excludes ATP.

cards.big: A forced-labour probe across 60 economies hands the US a second front. Every coalition chose patronage over reform.
cards.sub: Some China manufacturing adds minimal value to dodge tariffs. 7,200 local suppliers face scrutiny. Sabah and Sarawak bear full commodity exposure with zero chip plants.

cards.big: The court victory was a trap. Every government since Mahathir 1.0 chose procurement preferences over a US trade deal. Those consequences are arriving.
The vulnerability is real but leverage is underestimated. Malaysia lacks not options but political will to convert CPTPP, RCEP, and the NSSP into actual strategy.
